content:
# Leviticus Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 62v–63r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 93–94

---

**1** On the eighth day Moses called Aaron and his sons and the elders of Israel,

**2** and he said to Aaron, "Take for yourself a bull calf for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering, both without blemish, and offer them before the LORD.

**3** And to the people of Israel you shall speak, saying, 'Take a male goat for a sin offering, and a calf and a lamb, both a year old and without blemish, for a burnt offering,

**4** and an ox and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD, and a grain offering mixed with oil, for today the LORD will appear to you.'"

**5** And they brought what Moses commanded before the tent of meeting, and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD.

**6** And Moses said, "This is the thing that the LORD commanded you to do, that the glory of the LORD may appear to you."

**7** Then Moses said to Aaron, "Draw near to the altar and offer your sin offering and your burnt offering and make atonement for yourself and for the people, and bring the offering of the people and make atonement for them, as the LORD has commanded."

**8** So Aaron drew near to the altar and slaughtered the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself.

**9** And the sons of Aaron presented the blood to him, and he dipped his finger in the blood and put it on the horns of the altar and poured out the blood at the base of the altar.

**10** But the fat and the kidneys and the lobe of the liver from the sin offering he burned on the altar, as the LORD commanded Moses.

**11** The flesh and the hide he burned with fire outside the camp.

**12** Then he slaughtered the burnt offering, and Aaron's sons handed him the blood, and he threw it against the altar on all sides.

**13** And they handed the burnt offering to him piece by piece, and the head, and he burned them on the altar.

**14** And he washed the entrails and the legs and burned them on top of the burnt offering on the altar.

**15** Then he presented the people's offering. He took the goat of the sin offering that was for the people and slaughtered it and offered it for sin, like the first.

**16** And he presented the burnt offering and offered it according to the rule.

**17** And he presented the grain offering, took a handful of it, and burned it on the altar, besides the burnt offering of the morning.

**18** He also slaughtered the ox and the ram, the sacrifice of peace offerings for the people. And Aaron's sons handed him the blood, and he threw it against the altar on all sides.

**19** And the fat pieces of the ox and of the ram — the fat tail and the fat covering the entrails and the kidneys and the lobe of the liver —

**20** they put the fat pieces on the breasts, and he burned the fat pieces on the altar,

**21** but the breasts and the right thigh Aaron waved as a wave offering before the LORD, as Moses commanded.

**22** Then Aaron lifted up his hands toward the people and blessed them, and he came down from offering the sin offering and the burnt offering and the peace offerings.

**23** And Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting, and when they came out they blessed the people, and the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people.

**24** And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the fat pieces on the altar, and when all the people saw it, they shouted and fell on their faces.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 62v–63r.** Chapter 9 opens on folio 62v, following immediately after the close of chapter 8 (the seven-day ordination period) with a parashah break marking the structural and temporal transition to the eighth day. The parashah break before chapter 9 is a petucha (open section break), signaling a major narrative unit boundary — the conclusion of ordination and the beginning of public priestly ministry. This contrasts with the more tightly integrated internal structure of chapter 8, where the ordination ceremony is narrated without major internal breaks. The chapter runs from folio 62v through 63r. The Tiberian vocalization is consistent throughout. The verse count is twenty-four, and the chapter is narratively compact — structured as two parallel sequences (Aaron's own offerings, vv. 8–14; the people's offerings, vv. 15–21) framed by the divine command (vv. 1–7) and the climactic theophanic fire (vv. 22–24).

The structural refrain of chapter 8 — כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה (ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe — "as the LORD commanded Moses") — appears in chapter 9 at vv. 7 and 10, both in modified form: at v. 7 as כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה ("as the LORD commanded") and at v. 10 as כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה. The reduced frequency reflects the shift from instructional narration (ch. 8: Moses at every turn fulfilling divine commands) to active priestly execution (ch. 9: Aaron now performing, Moses receding). The word כָּבוֹד (kavod — "glory") appears at vv. 6 and 23, forming an inclusio: Moses announces that the glory of the LORD will appear (v. 6), and the chapter closes with the glory appearing and the fire falling (v. 23). The eighth day is thus framed as the day of the LORD's self-disclosure in glory.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 93–94 (LXX).** These two pages carry the entirety of LXX Leviticus 9. The chapter marker visible at the top of page 93 in the Vaticanus corresponds to the LXX's chapter enumeration of the priestly material. The uncial hand is continuous and legible throughout both pages, with no visible lacunae or major corrections in the photographic facsimile. The LXX text of chapter 9 is in general close agreement with the MT, with consistent terminological divergences in cultic vocabulary. Notable: the LXX at v. 4 adds a small interpretive expansion in the description of the grain offering; at v. 22 the LXX's rendering of Aaron's hand-lifting gesture is noteworthy; and the closing verse (v. 24) is in substantive agreement with the MT's dramatic theophanic conclusion, with the LXX preserving all the major narrative elements.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"A bull calf" / 'egel ben-baqar (v. 2).** Hebrew עֵגֶל בֶּן-בָּקָר (egel ben-baqar — "a calf, a son of the herd"). The term 'egel specifies a young bull — not the mature par (bull) of Lev 4:3 (the anointed priest's sin offering), but an 'egel, a calf. The distinction is significant: for his personal inauguration offering, Aaron offers a calf, not the full-grown bull that the text of Lev 4 prescribed for the high priest's ongoing inadvertent sin. Rabbinic tradition noted the significance of the calf and connected it to the golden calf incident of Exodus 32 — Aaron's public inauguration requires a calf-sin-offering as a form of atonement for his role in that event (cf. b. Avodah Zarah 2b). "A bull calf" renders 'egel ben-baqar by combining the two terms: 'egel (calf, young bull) and ben-baqar (son of the herd). Some translations render simply "a calf" (ESV) or "a young bull" (NIV). "A bull calf" (NASB) preserves both the youth ('egel) and the bovine category (ben-baqar) more precisely. The LXX renders μόσχον ἐκ βοῶν ("a calf of the oxen/cattle"), in close agreement.

2. **"For today the LORD will appear to you" / ki ha-yom YHWH nir'ah aleikhem (v. 4).** Hebrew כִּי הַיּוֹם יְהוָה נִרְאָה אֲלֵיכֶם (ki ha-yom YHWH nir'ah aleikhem — "for today the LORD will appear to you / for today the LORD has appeared to you"). The verb נִרְאָה (nir'ah) is the niphal (passive/reflexive) of ראה (ra'ah — "to see"): "to be seen, to appear, to allow oneself to be seen." The form is ambiguous between a prophetic perfect ("the LORD has appeared" — i.e., certain future spoken as accomplished) and a simple imperfect ("the LORD will appear"). Given the context — this is Moses' announcement before the offerings are made, and the theophanic fire comes only at the end of the chapter — a future rendering is most contextually accurate: "the LORD will appear to you." The LXX renders ὅτι σήμερον κύριος ὀφθήσεται ὑμῖν ("for today the LORD will be seen / will appear to you"), using the future passive ὀφθήσεται ("will be seen/appear") — supporting the future-tense reading. "Will appear" is retained.

3. **"The glory of the LORD" / kevod YHWH (vv. 6, 23).** Hebrew כְּבוֹד יְהוָה (kevod YHWH — "the weight/glory/honor of the LORD"). The kavod (כָּבוֹד) is the visible, tangible manifestation of YHWH's divine presence in his full luminous and weighty splendor. In Exodus, the kavod fills the tabernacle as a cloud (Exod 40:34–35). In Leviticus 9, the kavod "appears" to the people (v. 23) and is accompanied by the consuming fire (v. 24). The kavod is thus the visible form of YHWH's presence — a theophanic phenomenon accessible to human sight and described in terms of fire, cloud, and overwhelming weight of divine reality. "The glory of the LORD" renders kevod YHWH consistently throughout the series. The LXX renders ἡ δόξα κυρίου ("the glory of the LORD"), using δόξα ("glory, honor, splendor") for kavod, in standard agreement. The δόξα/kavod equivalence is among the most theologically significant translation decisions of the LXX translators.

4. **"Draw near to the altar" / geresh el-ha-mizbeach (v. 7).** Hebrew גְּשָׁה אֶל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (geshah el-ha-mizbeach — "draw near to the altar / approach the altar"). The verb נָגַשׁ (nagash) in the qal imperative means "to draw near, to approach, to come close." The act of drawing near to the altar is a technical priestly action — it is not permitted to everyone, and Aaron's drawing near (vayyiggash Aharon, v. 8) marks the formal beginning of his official priestly service. The same verb is used of Abraham (Gen 18:23), of Moses approaching God at Sinai (Exod 19:22), and elsewhere of the formal priestly approach to the altar. "Draw near to the altar" (ESV) renders geshah el-ha-mizbeach precisely. The LXX renders πρόσελθε πρὸς τὸ θυσιαστήριον ("approach / come forward to the altar"), using προσέρχομαι ("to come to, to approach"), in close agreement.

5. **"Make atonement for yourself and for the people" / kapper ba'adkha u-va'ad ha-am (v. 7).** Hebrew כַּפֵּר בַּעַדְךָ וּבְעַד הָעָם (kapper ba'adkha u-va'ad ha-am — "make atonement on behalf of yourself and on behalf of the people"). The verb כִּפֶּר (kapper, piel of kaphar) means to make atonement, to cover over, to ransom, to propitiate. It is the central cultic-priestly verb of Leviticus. The double structure of kapper ba'adkha u-va'ad ha-am — first for oneself, then for the people — reflects the theological principle that the atoning agent must himself be atoned for before he can atone for others (cf. Lev 16:6 on the Day of Atonement). Aaron's sin offering (the calf, vv. 2, 8–11) atones for Aaron himself; the people's sin offering (the male goat, vv. 3, 15) atones for the people. "Make atonement" renders kapper with the standard formal-equivalence rendering. The LXX renders ἐξίλασαι περὶ σεαυτοῦ καὶ περὶ τοῦ οἴκου σου ("propitiate/atone for yourself and for your house"), notably adding "your house" (τοῦ οἴκου σου) — a significant expansion not present in the MT of v. 7 (though the phrase "for himself and for his house" does appear in the Day of Atonement parallel, Lev 16:6, 11). This is a meaningful LXX plus.

6. **"Dipped his finger in the blood" / vayyitbol etsba'o ba-dam (v. 9).** Hebrew וַיִּטְבֹּל אֶצְבָּעוֹ בַּדָּם (vayyitbol etsba'o ba-dam — "and he dipped his finger in the blood"). The verb טָבַל (taval) means to dip, to immerse briefly. In the sin offering rite for the high priest (Lev 4:6, 17), the priest dips his finger in the blood and sprinkles it seven times before the veil. In chapter 9, the blood application is different: Aaron dips his finger and applies blood to the horns of the (burnt offering?) altar — not the sprinkling before the veil prescribed for the high priest's own sin in Lev 4:3–12, but the horn-touching rite prescribed for the communal sin offering (Lev 4:25). This reflects Aaron's status: his inaugural sin offering is treated procedurally like the communal sin offering (blood to the altar horns) rather than the high priestly sin offering (blood sprinkled before the inner veil). "Dipped his finger in the blood" renders vayyitbol etsba'o ba-dam precisely. The LXX renders ἔβαψεν τὸν δάκτυλον εἰς τὸ αἷμα ("he dipped the finger in the blood"), in close agreement, using βάπτω ("to dip, to immerse").

7. **"The lobe of the liver" / yoteret ha-kaved (v. 10).** Hebrew יֹתֶרֶת הַכָּבֵד (yoteret ha-kaved — "the appendage/excess of the liver / the lobe of the liver"). The yoteret is the caudate lobe (or the diaphragmatic lobe) of the animal's liver — a specific anatomical portion. The exact identification is disputed among anatomists and Talmudic commentators: it may be the caudate lobe, the hepatic appendage, or some protrusion of the liver. In any case, it is the specific portion of the liver designated for altar burning, not consumption. "The lobe of the liver" renders yoteret ha-kaved with the standard formal rendering, consistent with chapter 3 and chapter 8. The LXX renders τὸν λοβὸν τοῦ ἥπατος ("the lobe of the liver"), in direct agreement, using λοβός ("lobe") for yoteret.

8. **"Offered it for sin / like the first" / vaykhatte'hu ka-rishon (v. 15).** Hebrew וַיְחַטְּאֵהוּ כָּרִאשׁוֹן (vayykhatte'ehu ka-rishon — "and he performed the sin-offering procedure on it as the first one"). The verb vayykhatte'ehu (piel of חטא) is the same technical decontamination/purification verb as vayykhatte' in Lev 8:15 (the piel of chattat). Here it is applied to the goat sin offering of the people (v. 15): "he offered it for sin" is the idiomatic rendering of the piel in context. The phrase ka-rishon ("like the first") refers back to the first sin offering — Aaron's own calf sin offering of vv. 8–11. The procedural parallel is noted: the people's sin offering is handled according to the same rite as Aaron's. "Like the first" renders ka-rishon precisely. Some translations render "just as the first" (ESV) or "as with the first one" (NASB). The LXX renders καθὼς τὸ πρῶτον ("just as the first"), in close agreement.

9. **"According to the rule" / ka-mishpat (v. 16).** Hebrew כַּמִּשְׁפָּט (ka-mishpat — "according to the ordinance / according to the rule / as the judgment"). The mishpat (מִשְׁפָּט) is the prescribed procedure, the established ordinance, the rule. "According to the rule" renders ka-mishpat precisely and concisely. Some translations render "in the prescribed way" (NIV) or "according to the ordinance" (NASB). ESV: "according to the rule." The LXX renders κατὰ τὴν κρίσιν ("according to the judgment/ordinance"), using κρίσις ("judgment, decision, ordinance") for mishpat. This is a standard LXX equivalence for mishpat in cultic-legal contexts.

10. **"Took a handful of it" / vayyimale' kamtso mimmennu (v. 17).** Hebrew וַיְמַלֵּא כָמְצוֹ מִמֶּנּוּ (vayyemalle' kamtso mimmennu — "and he filled his fist/handful from it"). The kamats (כֹּמֶץ, or here in the qere form כְּמְצוֹ) is the fistful or handful — the ritual measure of grain offering removed for altar burning (the qomaç). Leviticus 2:2 established this gesture: the priest takes a handful (kamats, his open palm or fist) of the flour, oil, and frankincense from the grain offering and burns it as the memorial portion (azkarah) on the altar. The remainder belongs to Aaron and his sons (Lev 2:3). "Took a handful of it" renders vayyemalle' kamtso mimmennu idiomatically and accurately. The LXX renders καὶ ἔπλησεν τὴν χεῖρα αὐτοῦ ἀπ' αὐτῆς ("and he filled his hand from it"), using the general χεῖρα ("hand") rather than a specific word for the fistful measure.

11. **"Besides the burnt offering of the morning" / milvad olat ha-boqer (v. 17).** Hebrew מִלְּבַד עֹלַת הַבֹּקֶר (milvad olat ha-boqer — "besides / in addition to the burnt offering of the morning"). This is an important liturgical notation: the grain offering of v. 17 is offered on the altar in addition to the regular daily morning burnt offering (the tamid — the continual offering). Leviticus 9 thus presupposes the daily burnt offering system of Numbers 28:3–8, where a lamb is offered each morning and evening as the perpetual altar fire. The phrase milvad ("besides, apart from, in addition to") specifies that the offerings of the inaugural day are offered in addition to — not instead of — the regular daily offerings. "Besides the burnt offering of the morning" renders milvad olat ha-boqer precisely. The LXX renders χωρὶς τοῦ ὁλοκαυτώματος τοῦ πρωινοῦ ("apart from / besides the whole burnt offering of the morning"), in close agreement.

12. **"The fat pieces" / ha-chelev (vv. 19–20).** Hebrew הַחֵלֶב (ha-chelev — "the fat / the suet / the fat pieces"). As established throughout the sacrificial chapters (Lev 3, 4, 7, 8), chelev designates the specified ritual fat portions that belong exclusively to YHWH and are burned on the altar (Lev 3:16–17; 7:23–25). The prohibited chelev includes the fat covering the entrails, the fat tail (of the fat-tailed sheep), and the fat of the kidneys and liver. In vv. 19–20 these fat portions of the ox and ram of the peace offerings are listed and burned on the altar. "The fat pieces" renders ha-chelev in the context of the listed portions. Where a single item is in view, "the fat" is used; where the multiple portions are in view collectively, "the fat pieces" captures the plurality. The LXX renders τὸ στέαρ ("the fat/suet"), in standard agreement, using στέαρ ("fat, suet, grease") for chelev.

13. **"The fat tail" / ha-alyah (v. 19).** Hebrew הָאַלְיָה (ha-alyah — "the fat tail"). The alyah is the broad fat tail of certain Near Eastern sheep breeds (the Awassi/fat-tailed sheep) — a mass of fat that can weigh several kilograms and is used like butter in cooking. In the sacrificial law of Lev 3:9 and 7:3, the alyah is specified as one of the portions that must be removed and burned on the altar. It belongs to YHWH. "The fat tail" renders ha-alyah precisely. This is the rendering consistent with Lev 3 and 7. The LXX renders τὴν ὀσφύν ("the loin/hip"), which is a divergent rendering — the LXX appears not to know or use the specific fat-tail identification, generalizing to "the loin." This is a notable LXX divergence from the MT.

14. **"Aaron lifted up his hands toward the people and blessed them" / vayyissa' Aharon et-yadav el-ha-am vayvarekhem (v. 22).** Hebrew וַיִּשָּׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת-יָדָו אֶל-הָעָם וַיְבָרְכֵם (vayyissa' Aharon et-yadav el-ha-am vayyvarekhem — "and Aaron lifted up his hands to the people and blessed them"). The verb נָשָׂא (nasa') means "to lift, to raise, to bear." The gesture of "lifting up the hands" toward the people is the Aaronic posture of blessing — the priest raises both hands (yadav, a dual form — his two hands) toward the congregation and pronounces the blessing. This is the gesture associated with the Aaronic Benediction of Num 6:24–26 (though that text does not specify the hand-gesture explicitly). The high priestly hand-lifting as a gesture of blessing became the distinctive gesture of the Aaronic/Levitical priesthood, preserved in synagogue practice to this day by the kohanim (Priestly Blessing, duchaning). "Lifted up his hands toward the people" renders vayyissa' Aharon et-yadav el-ha-am accurately. The LXX renders ἐξάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸν λαόν ("having raised/lifted his hands upon the people"), in close agreement, using ἐξαίρω ("to raise up, to lift").

15. **"He came down from offering" / vayyered mi'asot (v. 22).** Hebrew וַיֵּרֶד מֵעֲשֹׂת (vayyered me'asot — "and he came down from doing / from having done"). The verb יָרַד (yarad) means "to go down, to descend." This implies that the altar on which Aaron was officiating was elevated — Aaron must literally descend (come down) from the altar platform after completing the offerings. The elevated altar (Exod 27:1–8; 38:1–7) was approached via a ramp (not steps — Exod 20:26) and was a raised structure. Aaron descends after completing the altar offerings before proceeding to join Moses at the tent of meeting (v. 23). "He came down from offering" renders vayyered me'asot precisely. The LXX renders καὶ κατέβη ("and he came down"), in agreement, using καταβαίνω ("to descend, to come down").

16. **"Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting" (v. 23).** Hebrew וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל-אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (vayyavo' Moshe ve-Aharon el-ohel mo'ed — "and Moses and Aaron went into the tent of meeting"). This verse is the only point in the Pentateuch where both Moses and Aaron enter the tent of meeting together. The theological significance is profound: Moses, who alone has previously entered the tent (cf. Exod 33:7–11; Lev 1:1), now enters together with Aaron — symbolically transferring the priestly access from the mediating prophet to the institutionalized priesthood. When they emerge together (vayyetse'u) and bless the people, the glory of the LORD appears — the divine endorsement of the transfer of priestly office. The text does not state what transpired inside; Jewish tradition (Rashi, following Sifra) suggests that Moses taught Aaron the laws of the incense altar inside the tent, which Aaron had not been present to hear. "Went into the tent of meeting" renders vayyavo' el-ohel mo'ed precisely. The LXX renders εἰσῆλθεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων εἰς τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("Moses and Aaron entered into the tent of witness/testimony"), using σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("tent of testimony/witness") for ohel mo'ed — the standard LXX rendering.

17. **"Fire came out from before the LORD" / vattetse' esh millifnei YHWH (v. 24).** Hebrew וַתֵּצֵא אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה (vattetse' esh millifnei YHWH — "and fire went out from before the LORD"). The theophanic fire is the chapter's climax — the divine confirmation of the priestly installation and the inauguration of Aaronic worship. The phrase millifnei YHWH ("from before the LORD") indicates that the fire originates from YHWH's direct presence — the same fire is described as coming from the cloud of divine presence in Num 16:35 (consuming Korah's followers) and from heaven in 2 Chr 7:1 (at Solomon's temple dedication). It is not the ordinary fire of the altar kindled by human agency; it is fire from YHWH himself. This fire from before the LORD establishes the paradigm for the altar fire that the priests are commanded never to extinguish (Lev 6:12–13). "Fire came out from before the LORD" renders vattetse' esh millifnei YHWH with precise formal equivalence. The LXX renders ἐξῆλθεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου ("fire went out from beside the LORD / from before the LORD"), using παρά + genitive ("from, from beside, from before"), in close agreement.

18. **"Consumed the burnt offering and the fat pieces" / vattokal et-ha-olah ve-et-ha-chelavaim (v. 24).** Hebrew וַתֹּאכַל אֶת-הָעֹלָה וְאֶת-הַחֲלָבִים (vattokal et-ha-olah ve-et-ha-chelavaim — "and it consumed the burnt offering and the fat pieces"). The verb אָכַל ('akhal) means "to eat, to consume, to devour." In the context of divine fire, the consuming of the sacrifice is described as the fire "eating" it — a vivid anthropomorphic idiom. The fire consumes specifically "the burnt offering and the fat pieces" — a summary reference to the altar contents at the time of the theophanic event. "Consumed" renders vattokal precisely. The LXX renders κατέφαγεν τὰ ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ τὰ στέατα ("it devoured/consumed the whole burnt offerings and the fat pieces"), using κατεσθίω ("to eat down, to devour, to consume entirely") — a more intensive compound verb — and ὁλοκαύτωμα for olah. The LXX's plural ὁλοκαυτώματα ("whole burnt offerings") treats the sacrificial category plurally, while the MT's olah is singular (the burnt offering as a category).

19. **"They shouted and fell on their faces" / vayyaronnu vayyippelu al-peneihem (v. 24).** Hebrew וַיָּרֹנּוּ וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל-פְּנֵיהֶם (vayyaronnu vayyippelu al-peneihem — "and they shouted/sang for joy and they fell on their faces"). The verb רָנַן (ranan) means "to give a ringing cry, to shout for joy, to cry out in exultation." It is used of joyful, exuberant vocalization — here the people's spontaneous corporate response to the theophanic fire. The immediate sequel — "and they fell on their faces" — indicates that the shout of joy is also an expression of reverence and awe before the overwhelming divine presence. Joy and fear before the holy are not opposites but simultaneous responses to the theophany. "Shouted" renders vayyaronnu with the vocalized, exultant sense of ranan; "fell on their faces" renders vayyippelu al-peneihem precisely — the posture of prostration before the divine. The LXX renders καὶ ἀνεβόησαν καὶ ἔπεσαν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ("and they cried out and fell upon the face"), using ἀναβοάω ("to cry out, to shout") for ranan. The LXX's ἀναβοάω captures the shouting/crying quality but loses the ringing joy-nuance of ranan; the response of falling on the face is precisely reproduced.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 93–94) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9:2 | 'egel ben-baqar ("a bull calf / a calf of the herd") | μόσχον ἐκ βοῶν ("a calf of the cattle/oxen") | Standard agreement. LXX μόσχος for 'egel throughout Leviticus. |
| 9:4 | ki ha-yom YHWH nir'ah aleikhem ("for today the LORD will appear to you") | ὅτι σήμερον κύριος ὀφθήσεται ὑμῖν ("for today the LORD will appear/be seen to you") | Agreement on future-tense rendering. LXX ὀφθήσεται (future passive of ὁράω) for MT niphal nir'ah. Both preserve the prospective force of the theophanic announcement. |
| 9:5 | va-yiqrevu kol-ha-edah ("and all the congregation drew near") | καὶ συνήχθη πᾶσα συναγωγή ("and all the assembly was gathered together") | LXX renders the drawing near as a gathering/assembling action (συνάγω — "to gather, to assemble"), rather than a drawing-near approach (LXX might use προσέρχομαι). The MT's emphasis is on the people approaching toward the tent; the LXX's emphasis is on the assembly congregating. |
| 9:7 | kapper ba'adkha u-va'ad ha-am ("make atonement for yourself and for the people") | ἐξίλασαι περὶ σεαυτοῦ καὶ περὶ τοῦ οἴκου σου ("atone for yourself and for your house") | Notable LXX plus. The MT reads "for yourself and for the people"; the LXX reads "for yourself and for your house" (τοῦ οἴκου σου). This aligns Lev 9:7 with the parallel Day of Atonement language of Lev 16:6, 11, where Aaron atones "for himself and for his house." The LXX may reflect a harmonizing tendency with Lev 16, or may attest a different Vorlage; or the "house" may have been lost from the MT tradition. The MT's "for the people" is the more inclusive reading and is followed in this translation. |
| 9:10 | yoteret ha-kaved ("the lobe of the liver") | τὸν λοβὸν τοῦ ἥπατος ("the lobe of the liver") | Full agreement. LXX λοβός for MT yoteret throughout. |
| 9:15 | vayykhatte'ehu ka-rishon ("and he offered it for sin like the first") | καθὼς τὸ πρῶτον ("just as the first") | Agreement. LXX omits the explicit verbal phrase "and he offered it for sin" and reads only "just as the first," condensing the MT's verbal clause into a comparative adverb. The MT's vayykhatte'ehu is the technical piel of chattat (the purification/sin-offering procedure verb); the LXX's condensation drops this specific verbal reference. |
| 9:16 | ka-mishpat ("according to the rule / according to the ordinance") | κατὰ τὴν κρίσιν ("according to the judgment/ordinance") | Standard agreement. LXX κρίσις for MT mishpat in legal/cultic contexts throughout. |
| 9:17 | milvad olat ha-boqer ("besides the burnt offering of the morning") | χωρὶς τοῦ ὁλοκαυτώματος τοῦ πρωινοῦ ("apart from the whole burnt offering of the morning") | Full agreement. LXX uses χωρίς ("apart from, besides") for milvad; ὁλοκαύτωμα for olah. |
| 9:19 | ha-alyah ("the fat tail") | τὴν ὀσφύν ("the loin/hip") | Significant divergence. The MT's ha-alyah specifically designates the broad fat tail of fat-tailed sheep — a well-defined sacrificial term (cf. Lev 3:9; 7:3). The LXX renders ὀσφύς ("loin, hip, lower back/waist region"), which does not correspond anatomically to the alyah. The LXX either did not have access to a fat-tailed sheep tradition that would make alyah meaningful, or reflects a different textual tradition, or generalizes the anatomical reference. This is one of the clearer instances where LXX cultic vocabulary diverges from the MT's precise sacrificial terminology. MT followed in this translation. |
| 9:21 | vayyanef Aharon otam tenufah ("and Aaron waved them as a wave offering") | ἀπήνεγκεν Ααρων ἐπίθεμα ἔναντι κυρίου ("Aaron brought/carried as a placed-offering before the LORD") | Standard LXX rendering: ἐπίθεμα for tenufah, as throughout Leviticus. The waving gesture (MT נוּף nuf) is rendered as a bringing-before gesture in the LXX. See note on Lev 8:27 for full discussion. |
| 9:22 | vayyissa' Aharon et-yadav el-ha-am vayvarekhem ("and Aaron lifted up his hands toward the people and blessed them") | ἐξάρας τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸν λαόν, εὐλόγησεν αὐτούς ("having raised his hands upon the people, he blessed them") | The LXX uses ἐπί + accusative ("upon the people") where the MT uses אֶל ("toward / to the people"). The directional nuance differs slightly: MT's el ("toward") suggests the hands pointed in the direction of the people; LXX's ἐπί ("upon") suggests the hands extended over the people. Both are consistent with the priestly blessing posture. The LXX's εὐλόγησεν ("blessed") directly renders MT's vayvarekhem ("and he blessed them"). |
| 9:23 | ohel mo'ed ("tent of meeting") | τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("the tent of testimony/witness") | Standard LXX rendering: σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου for ohel mo'ed throughout. The LXX consistently renders mo'ed ("meeting, appointed time") with μαρτύριον ("testimony, witness"), reflecting the LXX's interpretation of the tent as a place of divine witness/testimony rather than a meeting-place. |
| 9:23 | kevod YHWH ("the glory of the LORD") | ἡ δόξα κυρίου ("the glory of the LORD") | Standard agreement. δόξα for kavod is among the most consistent and theologically significant equivalences in the LXX. |
| 9:24 | vattetse' esh millifnei YHWH ("and fire came out from before the LORD") | ἐξῆλθεν πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου ("fire went out from beside/from before the LORD") | Agreement in substance. LXX uses παρά + genitive ("from beside, from from the presence of") for millifnei ("from before the face of"). The distinction is slight: MT millifnei YHWH ("from before the face/presence of YHWH") carries a more explicitly personal/facial imagery; LXX παρὰ κυρίου ("from the LORD" or "from beside the LORD") is slightly less personal but functionally equivalent. Both indicate divine origin of the fire. |
| 9:24 | vattokal et-ha-olah ve-et-ha-chelavaim ("and it consumed the burnt offering and the fat pieces") | κατέφαγεν τὰ ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ τὰ στέατα ("it devoured the whole burnt offerings and the fat pieces") | LXX pluralizes olah ("the burnt offering" → "the whole burnt offerings"), treating the offering type as a collective plural. LXX uses κατεσθίω ("to devour/consume entirely") for MT אָכַל ('akhal — "to eat/consume") — a more intensive compound form. |
| 9:24 | vayyaronnu vayyippelu al-peneihem ("and they shouted and fell on their faces") | καὶ ἀνεβόησαν καὶ ἔπεσαν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ("and they cried out and fell upon the face") | Agreement in structure. LXX ἀναβοάω ("to cry out aloud") for MT רָנַן (ranan — "to give a ringing cry of joy"), and πίπτω ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ("to fall on the face") for vayyippelu al-peneihem. The LXX loses the joyful-exultation nuance of ranan (which has a distinctly celebratory/jubilant quality beyond a mere cry or shout) but preserves the physical prostration precisely. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 9)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| עֵגֶל בֶּן-בָּקָר ('egel ben-baqar) | a bull calf | A young bull; LXX: μόσχος ἐκ βοῶν |
| כְּבוֹד יְהוָה (kevod YHWH) | the glory of the LORD | Theophanic presence-manifestation; LXX: ἡ δόξα κυρίου |
| כַּמִּשְׁפָּט (ka-mishpat) | according to the rule | Prescribed procedure; LXX: κατὰ τὴν κρίσιν |
| כֹּמֶץ (kamats) | a handful | The ritual fistful of grain offering; LXX: χεῖρα ("hand") |
| הָאַלְיָה (ha-alyah) | the fat tail | The broad fat tail of fat-tailed sheep; LXX: τὴν ὀσφύν ("the loin") — divergent |
| מִלְּבַד (milvad) | besides | "In addition to"; LXX: χωρίς |
| וַיְחַטְּאֵהוּ (vayykhatte'ehu, piel) | offered it for sin | Technical sin-offering decontamination procedure; LXX: condensed into comparative adverb |
| כַּפֵּר (kapper, piel) | make atonement | Central priestly cultic verb; LXX: ἐξιλάσκομαι |
| וַיִּשָּׂא ... אֶת-יָדָו (vayyissa' et-yadav) | lifted up his hands | Priestly blessing gesture; LXX: ἐξάρας τὰς χεῖρας |
| רָנַן (ranan) | shouted | Joyful exultant cry; LXX: ἀναβοάω ("cried out") |
| וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל-פְּנֵיהֶם (vayyippelu al-peneihem) | fell on their faces | Prostration before the divine; LXX: ἔπεσαν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον |
| אֵשׁ מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה (esh millifnei YHWH) | fire from before the LORD | Theophanic fire of divine origin; LXX: πῦρ παρὰ κυρίου |
| שְׁלָמִים (shelamim) | peace offerings | Fellowship/wholeness offerings; established from Lev 3 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2 |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4 |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | LXX: σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אַהֲרֹן (Aharon) | Aaron | English name form |
| מֹשֶׁה (Moshe) | Moses | English name form |
| יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisrael) | Israel | English name form |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | a pleasing aroma | Formulaic divine acceptance idiom; LXX: εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Any altar-consumed offering; LXX Vaticanus: κάρπωμα |
| תְּנוּפָה (tenufah) | wave offering | Ritual presentation gesture; LXX: ἐπίθεμα |
| הַחֵלֶב (ha-chelev) | the fat / the fat pieces | Specified altar-fat portions; LXX: τὸ στέαρ |
| יֹתֶרֶת הַכָּבֵד (yoteret ha-kaved) | the lobe of the liver | Caudate lobe; LXX: ὁ λοβὸς τοῦ ἥπατος |

— LOGOS
